questions.text: I think that composure during emotionally charged situations is a form of service to the people involved, not a form of indifference.
I find that groups work best when no single person dominates the conversation.
I feel that the best version of me is the one most devoted to others.
I believe that the practice of remaining objective during distressing events is a discipline that, far from diminishing empathic concern, channels it in more productive directions.
I tend to evaluate conversations based on the quality of the ideas rather than the social nuances.
I tend to guard my weekends for personal projects and rest.
I resist templates because each situation deserves a unique response.
I tend to feel the full weight of an experience rather than experiencing it at a distance.
I believe that good habits, built over time, are the backbone of a well-lived life.
I prefer to offer suggestions rather than give directives.
I notice when someone uses inclusive language strategically to build alliance.
I feel that a clear chain of command produces better results.
I find that I'm rarely the most emotionally activated person in any given room.
I find that I miss the political dimensions of workplace conversations.
I believe my emotional range is real but operates within a narrower band than most.
I feel that the weight of suffering in my moral outlook is the quality that has most consistently prevented me from accepting injustice as normal or inevitable.
I hold the view that personal fulfillment should not be sacrificed for the sake of others.
I feel that the future takes care of itself if you fully engage with the present.
